agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request introduces a 'confirmed' status for quotes, but the term 'confirmed' is already established in the Orders bounded context with a fundamentally different meaning. This creates a direct domain language collision.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Quoting — the term 'confirmed' is being shared across two contexts with incompatible semantics.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. It is immutable — no further changes allowed."},{"term":"Confirmed Quote (proposed)","definition":"A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT yet occurred. Mutable (payment still pending)."},{"term":"Immutable","definition":"Once an entity reaches this state, its core attributes (line items, pricing, terms) cannot be modified."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A pre-order document representing proposed terms and pricing offered to a customer."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A binding commercial transaction that proceeds through fulfillment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A 'confirmed' order means payment has been received and the order is queued for fulfillment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an established, in-use definition tied to payment state and fulfillment triggers. Violating it corrupts downstream financial and logistics processes.","testable_assertion":"Every order in 'confirmed' status has an associated successful payment record."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A confirmed order is immutable — no modifications to line items, pricing, or terms after confirmation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Immutability after payment protects financial integrity and prevents fulfillment of mismatched goods.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to modify a confirmed order's line items, pricing, or terms is rejected by the system."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A 'confirmed' quote (proposed) means verbal agreement only — payment has NOT occurred.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a proposed business policy for a new workflow state, not yet established. A VP could redefine quote lifecycle states.","testable_assertion":"A quote in 'confirmed' status has no associated payment record."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The term 'confirmed' must have a single, unambiguous meaning within the domain language to prevent miscommunication between teams, systems, and reports.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Ubiquitous language consistency is a foundational invariant. If 'confirmed' means 'paid' in one context and 'unpaid' in another, cross-context communication (reports, APIs, dashboards, support) will produce dangerous misinterpretations.","testable_assertion":"Searching the codebase and documentation for 'confirmed' yields entities that share the same semantic meaning regarding payment state."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order exists in 'confirmed' status","when":"Any system component or stakeholder references its 'confirmed' state","then":"It is universally understood that payment has been received and the order is immutable"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms","when":"A sales agent records this agreement","then":"The quote transitions to a status whose name does NOT collide with the established meaning of 'confirmed' in the Orders context"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Any reporting, API, or dashboard that filters by 'confirmed' status","when":"Results are returned","then":"All results share the same semantic meaning — there is no ambiguity about whether payment has occurred"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A support agent sees a 'confirmed' quote and assumes payment was received, telling the customer their order is being fulfilled.","expected_behavior":"This must never happen. The quote status name must be distinct enough to prevent confusion with confirmed orders."},{"scenario":"A report aggregates all 'confirmed' entities across orders and quotes, conflating paid and unpaid items.","expected_behavior":"Cross-entity reporting must not produce misleading financial totals. Distinct status names prevent this."},{"scenario":"A quote is 'confirmed' (verbally agreed) but the customer never pays. The quote lingers in 'confirmed' status indefinitely.","expected_behavior":"The quote lifecycle should handle expiration or follow-up for verbally-agreed-but-unpaid quotes — but this is secondary to resolving the naming collision."},{"scenario":"An integration or webhook fires on 'confirmed' status change, triggering fulfillment for a quote that has no payment.","expected_behavior":"Status-based automations must not accidentally trigger order-fulfillment workflows for unpaid quotes."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk. If 'confirmed' is overloaded, fulfillment could be triggered on unpaid quotes, shipping goods without payment. Reporting could conflate paid revenue with verbal agreements, distorting financial statements.","operational":"Dangerous ambiguity across teams. Operations, support, finance, and engineering would need to constantly disambiguate which 'confirmed' they mean. This is a guaranteed source of production incidents.","user":"Sales agents need a way to record verbal agreement — the underlying need is valid. But the naming collision creates confusion in every downstream system they interact with.","risk":"Critical. Domain language corruption is one of the highest-impact risks in a growing system. It compounds over time as more features, reports, and integrations build on the ambiguous term.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Need to record verbal agreement — legitimate need, but the status name must not collide with order terminology."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"Revenue recognition depends on 'confirmed' meaning 'paid.' Overloading this term could corrupt financial reporting."},{"group":"Operations / Fulfillment","impact":"Fulfillment triggers on 'confirmed' status. A quote hitting 'confirmed' could accidentally trigger shipping."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Agents referencing 'confirmed' status must know unambiguously whether payment occurred."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Every status-based query, event handler, and automation touching 'confirmed' becomes a potential bug surface."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction: 'confirmed' is explicitly defined as paid+immutable for orders, and the feature request explicitly defines it as unpaid+verbal-agreement for quotes. These two definitions cannot coexist under the same term without corrupting the ubiquitous language. Confidence is low because shipping this as-is would be incorrect.","high_confidence_areas":["The existing definition of 'confirmed' for orders is clear and established","The underlying business need (recording verbal agreement on quotes) is valid","The term collision is unambiguous — paid vs unpaid is a direct contradiction"],"low_confidence_areas":[],"assumptions":["The existing 'confirmed' order definition is actively in use and cannot be changed","No other bounded contexts already use 'confirmed' with yet another meaning"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The term 'confirmed' has an established meaning of 'paid and immutable' in the Orders context. The feature request defines 'confirmed' for quotes as 'verbally agreed, payment NOT yet occurred.' These are directly contradictory on payment state (paid vs unpaid) and mutability (immutable vs still in progress). Using the same term for both will corrupt the ubiquitous language.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Agents","Finance","Operations","Customer Support","Engineering"],"options":["A) Rename the quote status to something unambiguous like 'accepted' or 'agreed' — meaning the customer accepted the terms verbally, distinguishing it from 'confirmed' which means paid.","B) Rename the quote status to 'customer-approved' to emphasize it is the customer's verbal approval, not a system confirmation of payment.","C) Rename the existing order status away from 'confirmed' — NOT recommended as it is already established and in use."],"recommendation":"Option A: Use 'accepted' for quotes. It accurately describes the business event (customer accepted terms), avoids collision with 'confirmed' (which means paid+immutable), and is intuitive for sales agents. The underlying need is fully supported — only the name changes."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Reusing the term 'confirmed' with contradictory semantics across orders and quotes","business_rule_broken":"BR-4: Ubiquitous language consistency. 'Confirmed' cannot simultaneously mean 'paid and immutable' (orders) and 'verbally agreed, unpaid' (quotes). This will cause financial reporting errors, fulfillment misfires, and cross-team miscommunication."}]}

This creates a direct domain language collision.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Quoting — the term 'confirmed' is being shared across two contexts with incompatible semantics.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"An order that has been paid and

content.thinking:
This is a classic domain language contradiction. The term "confirmed" is being overloaded with two different meanings across two contexts (orders vs quotes), and they have contradictory semantics around payment state.

Let me analyze and produce the JSON output.

The key contradiction: "confirmed" on an order means paid and immutable. "confirmed" on a quote means verbally agreed but NOT paid. Same term, opposite payment semantics. This is a domain language collision that will cause confusion.

Escalation type: contradiction — two explicit definitions directly conflict on the meaning of "confirmed."
